result: I’ve been thinking about the way light pools on the kitchen table at 3:17 p.m., exactly when the sun clears the neighbor’s gable and slips through the thin place where the curtains never quite close. It lands on the remains of lunch: a crust of bread, the knife still buttered, a glass with one swallow of water trembling like mercury. In that moment the room feels embarrassed, caught in the middle of an honest sentence it didn’t mean to say aloud. I stand there, hand on the fridge door, forgetting what I came for, hypnotized by the brightness that insists everything ordinary is also perishable. The breadcrumbs become a map, the knife a silver bridge, the glass a lens concentrating the afternoon into a bead hot enough to burn if I touched it. I never do. Instead I memorize the temperature of the silence, the way it tastes of yeast and tin, the way it expands until the walls bulge like lungs holding a secret breath. Somewhere outside, a dog rehearses the same three notes of complaint. Somewhere inside, the clock ticks once and then again, and the light, without permission, moves three inches left, erasing the map, collapsing the bridge, cooling the lens. Nothing has happened, yet everything I thought I knew about the room has been quietly re-written in a language of shine and shadow. I close the fridge, leave the dishes, walk backwards out of the scene so the table can finish its confession without me.

In that moment the room feels embarrassed, caught in the middle of an honest sentence it didn’t mean to say aloud. I stand there, hand on the fridge door, forgetting what I came for, hypnotized by the brightness that insists everything ordinary is also perishable. The breadcrumbs become a map, the knife a silver bridge, the glass a lens concentrating the afternoon into a bead hot enough to burn if I touched it. I never do. Instead I memorize the temperature of the silence, the way it tastes of yeast and tin, the way it expands until the walls bulge like lungs holding a secret breath. Somewhere outside, a dog rehearses the same three notes of complaint. Somewhere inside, the clock ticks once and then again, and the light, without permission, moves three inches left, erasing the map, collapsing the bridge, cooling the lens. Nothing has happened, yet everything I thought I knew about the room has been quietly re-written in a language of shine and shadow. I close the fridge, leave the dishes, walk backwards out of the scene so the table can finish its confession without me.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
